title: US-Iran war: Construction cost impacts to linger
gist: A US-Iran ceasefire MoU reopens the Strait of Hormuz and eases oil-shock risk, but Oxford Economics argues Australian construction costs will stay elevated because the oil premium is already embedded in wages, bitumen/diesel prices, procurement and risk margins, and because damaged Gulf refining infrastructure (Qatar LNG, Saudi Ras Tanura, UAE Fujairah) prolongs energy disruption beyond crude price moves.

insights.claim: The US-Iran memorandum of understanding extends the ceasefire by 60 days and reopens the Strait of Hormuz, lowering immediate energy-shock risk, but does not reset construction costs that have already absorbed the oil premium.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Don't fade construction-cost inflation on the ceasefire headline; embedded costs unwind slowly, so cost-escalation exposure persists for contractors and infrastructure clients.
insights.quote: the ceasefire matters, but it does not immediately remove the oil premium from Australian construction.

insights.claim: Construction is more exposed to oil and energy markets than most other sectors via fuel for equipment, oil-based materials (bitumen, plastics, resins), and energy-intensive inputs (steel, cement, glass).
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Use oil-intensity as the screen for which building-materials and contractor names carry the most margin risk from energy spikes.

themes.theme: Hard cost data: diesel and bitumen pass-through

insights.claim: Australian diesel prices in the June quarter were approximately 50% higher than the previous quarter, evidencing direct oil pass-through into construction inputs.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: A 50% QoQ diesel jump is a near-term margin hit for fuel-heavy contractors even with crude now easing.
insights.quote: Australian diesel prices were approximately 50% higher than in the previous quarter
insights.locator: Using the latest available June-quarter pricing

insights.claim: June-quarter bitumen prices were around 34% higher than the March quarter across Queensland, Victoria and Western Australia.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Road-project budgets are most exposed; watch margin compression in road-building contractors with locked-in tender prices.
insights.quote: June quarter prices around 34% higher than in the March quarter

insights.claim: Diesel and road freight account for around 5% of total costs on a typical infrastructure project, while for road projects diesel plus bitumen combined can exceed 20% of costs.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Quantifies oil sensitivity by project type: road builders face >20% cost exposure to oil-linked inputs, the highest-risk segment.
insights.quote: the combined share of diesel and bitumen can exceed 20%.
insights.locator: These inputs represent a meaningful share

themes.theme: Refinery damage prolongs the shock beyond crude

insights.claim: Qatar's LNG capacity has been largely shut down at the Ras Laffan hub, with fires and operational issues reported at Saudi Arabia's Ras Tanura refining complex and energy installations in Fujairah, UAE.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Physical infrastructure damage, not just transit risk, underpins energy prices; LNG and refined-product spreads may stay wide even after Hormuz reopens.
insights.quote: Qatar's LNG capacity has been largely shut down at the Ras Laffan industrial hub
insights.locator: A defining feature of the conflict

insights.claim: Attacks on refineries and processing infrastructure could prolong market disruption more than crude shortages alone, because restarting a forced-shutdown refinery takes a phased process of up to several weeks and repairs longer still.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Even with the Strait reopening and oil flowing, refined-product (diesel, bitumen) tightness persists longer than crude, favoring downstream crack-spread strength.
insights.quote: energy prices may remain elevated while damaged infrastructure is repaired and normal operating capacity is restored.
insights.locator: The bottom line is that attacks on refineries

themes.theme: Cost rigidities: wages, procurement, risk margins

insights.claim: Construction wage outcomes locked in for several years through enterprise agreements and awards mean higher labour costs persist even after the energy shock fades, since agreed wages rarely reverse and at best plateau.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Labour cost ratchet makes construction cost inflation structurally sticky; falling oil won't translate to falling project costs near-term.
insights.quote: Once wage outcomes are agreed, they rarely reverse. At best, they tend to plateau.

insights.claim: Contractors, suppliers and financiers are unlikely to remove risk contingencies immediately after conditions improve, and elevated inflation may keep financing costs higher for longer.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Risk premia and financing costs lag the de-escalation, extending project feasibility hurdles even as geopolitics calm.
insights.quote: Risk pricing can also prove sticky.
insights.locator: Risk pricing can also prove sticky

insights.claim: Oxford Economics' baseline assumes trade flows recover gradually July-September 2026 and normalize by March 2027, while a downside scenario delays recovery by about six months with Brent peaking far higher and not normalizing until at least 2029.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Defines the bull/bear oil path: a prolonged-conflict tail keeps oil-linked costs elevated multi-year, a key risk for long-dated infrastructure budgets.
insights.quote: Brent crude oil prices rise to significantly higher peaks and remain elevated for much longer, not returning to normality until at least 2029.

insights.claim: Global supply chains have coped via reconfigured routes and drawn-down reserves, exemplified by China importing US and Russian feedstock to sustain resin exports while Gulf production is constrained, but these buffers are limited.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Buffer depletion is the trigger for a sharper price escalation; monitor reserve drawdowns and resin/feedstock flows as the leading stress indicator.
insights.quote: China has been able to step in by importing feedstock from US and Russian reserves, helping to prevent a sharper escalation in prices.
insights.locator: Resin exports are a useful example

insights.claim: Smaller subcontractors face the greatest pressure where input costs rise after prices have already been agreed, making oil-linked exposure rather than the oil price itself the practical risk.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Credit/counterparty risk concentrates in fixed-price small subcontractors; a watch item for project default and dispute risk down the supply chain.
insights.quote: Smaller subcontractors may also face greater pressure where costs rise after prices have already been agreed.
insights.locator: That makes exposure the practical issue
